title: Controlled Vocabulary: Story Element Place Types
description: Canonical set of place types used across archetype element templates. Every place in an element template or instance must be assigned exactly one type. These types describe narrative function of a location — how it serves the story's structure — not its physical characteristics.

place_types.definition: The protagonist's baseline environment — the known, stable, everyday reality before the story's central conflict begins.
place_types.structural_function: Establishes the starting state against which all change is measured. Appears at Origin nodes and often at Resolution.
place_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Shire (The Quest), Tatooine (Hero's Journey), The Family Home (Coming of Age), The Pre-Transformation Life (Rebirth)

place_types.definition: A boundary space marking the transition between the known world and the unknown — a literal or figurative doorway.
place_types.structural_function: Marks the point of no return. Appears at Threshold nodes. Crossing it signals commitment to the journey.
place_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Edge of the Forest (The Quest), The Airport Departure Gate (Voyage and Return), The Prison Wall (The Escape)

place_types.definition: The unfamiliar domain where the story's central action unfolds — governed by different rules than the ordinary world.
place_types.structural_function: Primary arena for trials, revelations, and transformation. Most archetype nodes from Threshold through Crisis occur here.
place_types.examples_across_archetypes: Oz (Voyage and Return), The Underworld (Hero's Journey), The Royal Court (Rags to Riches), The Monster's Domain (Overcoming the Monster)

place_types.definition: A temporary place of safety, rest, or preparation within the special world — a reprieve from the central conflict.
place_types.structural_function: Provides breathing room for planning, healing, or reflection. Often where mentor encounters or ally-bonding occurs.
place_types.examples_across_archetypes: Rivendell (The Quest), The Rebel Base (Hero's Journey), The Safe House (The Escape), The Hidden Camp (Overcoming the Monster)

place_types.definition: The antagonist's seat of power — the place where opposing forces are concentrated and the protagonist is most vulnerable.
place_types.structural_function: Site of Crisis and climactic confrontation. Entering it represents maximum risk. Often the final obstacle.
place_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Death Star (Hero's Journey), The Dragon's Lair (Overcoming the Monster), The Tyrant's Castle (The Escape), Dunsinane (Tragedy)

place_types.definition: A place of desolation, exhaustion, or spiritual emptiness — reflecting the protagonist's lowest point.
place_types.structural_function: Externalizes internal despair. Appears at Descent or low-point nodes. The environment mirrors the character's state.
place_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Desert Crossing (The Quest), The Aftermath of Battle (Overcoming the Monster), The Ruined Kingdom (Rise and Fall)

place_types.definition: A place where paths diverge and the protagonist must choose a direction — literally or figuratively.
place_types.structural_function: Site of Commitment or key decision nodes. The physical branching mirrors narrative branching.
place_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Fork in the Road (The Quest), The Marketplace of Options (Comedy), The Moral Dilemma Setting (The Sacrifice)

place_types.definition: The deepest, most dangerous layer of the story's world — a place of death, darkness, or fundamental truth.
place_types.structural_function: Site of the supreme ordeal or ultimate revelation. Going here and returning is the story's deepest transformation.
place_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Cave (Hero's Journey), The Mines of Moria (The Quest), The Psychological Abyss (The Transformation)

place_types.definition: The highest point of achievement, clarity, or power — where the protagonist's arc reaches its peak before resolution.
place_types.structural_function: Site of triumph or tragic height before fall. May represent both physical elevation and maximum agency.
place_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Throne Room (Rags to Riches), The Peak of Power (Rise and Fall), The Moment of Victory (Overcoming the Monster)

place_types.definition: The place of return — the ordinary world revisited, now seen through changed eyes. May be the same location as ordinary_world but transformed in meaning.
place_types.structural_function: Site of Resolution. Shows what has changed by returning to what was known. The contrast measures the journey's impact.
place_types.examples_across_archetypes: The Shire (Return) (The Quest), Home After War (Voyage and Return), The New Life (Rebirth), Scrooge's Chambers (Rebirth)
